channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Times/Siena Poll Shows Hannah Pingree Leading in Maine Governor’s Race
item.description: Hannah Pingree, former Maine House speaker, starts the race to Election Day against Bobby Charles in a strong position, according to a New York Times/Portland Press Herald/Siena poll.
item.dc:creator: Evan Reynolds

item.title: Word of the Day: expatiate
item.description: This word has appeared in two articles on NYTimes.com in the past year. Can you use it in a sentence?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: Moscow Is Attacked by Dozens of Drones, Its Mayor Says
item.description: Sergei Sobyanin said the Russian military had shot down more than 60 drones. He did not report any injuries.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon

media:content.media:credit: Anastasia Barashkova/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Russian security personnel in central Moscow on Monday.

item.title: Mideast Live Updates: U.S. and Iran Offer Conflicting Plans for Doha Talks
item.description: Iranian officials contradicted President Trump’s claim that representatives from the two countries would speak directly in Qatar’s capital on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Norway’s “Viking Row” Craze Takes Over World Cup
item.description: Norway’s “Viking Row” cheer, where fans mimic the rowing of a longship while chanting, “Ro!” has taken over stadiums, schools and parliament during the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Jiwoong Hong

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Norway fans at a World Cup group stage match against Senegal in East Rutherford, N.J., on June 22.

item.title: Para revelar las raíces rítmicas de la risa, basta con hacerle cosquillas a un simio
item.description: Un estudio realizado con chimpancés, gorilas y otros grandes simios, además de niños humanos, arroja luz sobre la evolución de la risa.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

media:content.media:credit: Emil Lippe for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: All the planet’s great apes laugh, and they often do so in the same kind of regular, repeating rhythm that humans do, according to a new study.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for June 30, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Columbia University Has a New President. Again. This One Plans to Stay.
item.description: Jennifer Mnookin will be the Ivy League university’s fifth president in four years. She describes herself as “a principled pragmatist.”
item.dc:creator: Sharon Otterman

media:content.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Jennifer Mnookin, who begins her tenure on Wednesday, will be the 21st president of Columbia University.

item.title: Trying to Keep Track of Mangione’s State Case? It May Not Be Easy.
item.description: Though public access is a right in both federal and state courts, the judge in Luigi Mangione’s New York case has kept some legal documents sealed.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Benjamin Weiser

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by Angelina Katsanis
media:content.media:description: Luigi Mangione, center, at a pretrial hearing in court in June, when a judge announced Mr. Mangione’s lawyers were considering “extreme emotional disturbance” as a defense. His legal team withdrew the plan a day later.

item.title: John Oliver Dives Into Trump’s Pet Project: A ‘Giant Duck Death Trap’
item.description: The “Last Week Tonight” host said the United States was “dealing with a literal swamp of corruption” for its 250th birthday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Trump cerró un acuerdo minero y sus hijos se beneficiarán
item.description: Un acuerdo entre EE. UU. y Kazajistán ha dado a un grupo de inversores vinculados al presidente y al secretario de Comercio acceso a una de las mayores reservas de tungsteno sin explotar del mundo.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Eric Lipton

media:content.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Alibek Kazbekuly, de Kaz Resources, examina unas rocas en uno de los yacimientos de tungsteno.

item.title: La hipocresía de la Corte Suprema
item.description: El máximo tribunal de Estados Unidos está otorgando nuevos poderes a un presidente que con frecuencia actúa como un aspirante a autócrata.
item.dc:creator: El Comité Editorial

media:content.media:credit: Ilustración fotográfica por Aleksey Kondratyev para The New York Times

item.title: Venezuela’s Tragedy Is Bigger Than the Earthquakes
item.description: Venezuela’s natural disaster was unavoidable, but the devastation it has left in its wake was not.
item.dc:creator: Carolina Jiménez Sandoval

media:content.media:credit: Matias Delacroix/Associated Press

item.title: Pope Leo’s Showdown With the Church’s Radical Right
item.description: An impending clash between Pope Leo and ultratraditionalist Catholics threatens to end the pontiff’s honeymoon.
item.dc:creator: David Gibson

media:content.media:credit: Paola Chapdelaine for The New York Times

item.title: ¿Por qué los aficionados de Noruega hacen un ‘remo vikingo’ en el Mundial?
item.description: Multitudes de aficionados noruegos vestidos de rojo reman codo con codo en estadios, colegios e incluso en una residencia de ancianos. No todo el mundo está encantado.
item.dc:creator: Louise Krüger and Lynsey Chutel

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Hinchas de Noruega en un partido de la fase de grupos del Mundial contra Senegal, celebrado en East Rutherford, Nueva Jersey, el 22 de junio

item.title: Norway’s ‘Viking Row’ Is a Fan Phenomenon at the World Cup
item.description: Seas of red-clad Norwegian supporters are rowing shoulder to shoulder at stadiums, schools, even a nursing home. Not everyone is thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Louise Krüger and Lynsey Chutel

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Norway fans at a World Cup group stage match against Senegal in East Rutherford, N.J., on June 22.

item.title: Quote of the Day: A Viking Nation Pulls Together
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Tuesday, June 30, 2026.

item.title: NYT Crossword Answers for June 30, 2026
item.description: John Liber makes his New York Times Crossword debut.
item.dc:creator: Sam Corbin

media:content.media:credit: Gary Tramontina for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Increase your chances of solving today’s crossword by reading the Wordplay column.

item.title: Daveigh Chase, voz de ‘Lilo & Stitch’, murió a causa del sida
item.description: Chase era la voz de Lilo y también interpretó a la niña villana en la película de terror “El aro”. Falleció en Los Ángeles este mes.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens

media:content.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Getty Images North America
media:content.media:description: La actriz Daveigh Chase en 2011. Su padre dijo que se encontraba sin hogar antes de su muerte este mes en Los Ángeles.

item.title: Former Chinese Billionaire and Bannon Associate Is Sentenced for Fraud
item.description: The businessman courted U.S. conservatives as an anti-Communist crusader while using his supporters’ money to buy lavish homes and a Bugatti supercar.
item.dc:creator: Michael Forsythe

media:content.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Guo Wengui at his Manhattan apartment in 2017.

item.title: In Michigan, the Most Moderate Candidate May Not Be the Most Electable
item.description: The case for Haley Stevens’s candidacy rests on electability, but it’s far from clear how electable she really is.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg

media:content.media:credit: Bill Clark/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Haley Stevens

item.title: Judge Orders Trump to End Efforts to Kill Hudson Tunnel Funding
item.description: A federal judge says that the administration’s attempts to block money for the $16 billion rail project were “flagrantly” illegal.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan

media:content.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Gateway tunnel project will add a rail tunnel under the Hudson River, supplementing 116-year-old single-track tunnels.

item.title: California Hopes to Speed Up Election Results With $40 Million Infusion
item.description: It took six days to determine the outcome of the primary for Los Angeles mayor, and seven for the governor’s race.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall

media:content.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: California will spend $40 million to help counties accelerate their ballot counts in November. Some of the money will encourage voters to participate earlier if they plan to use mail ballots.

item.title: Director Who Defrauded Netflix Gets 30-Month Prison Term
item.description: Carl Rinsch was convicted last year of stealing $11 million from the streamer, which funded a science fiction series he had pitched.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou

media:content.media:credit: Seth Wenig/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Carl Rinsch leaving federal court in Manhattan on Monday after a judge, citing Mr. Rinsch’s mental health issues, sentenced him to half the prosecution’s recommended term.

item.title: Two Dan Sullivans to Appear on Alaska Senate Ballot, State High Court Rules
item.description: Republicans had sought the removal of a little-known candidate with the same name as the incumbent senator, arguing that he was not a “good faith” candidate.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning

media:content.media:credit: Katie Holmlund/Associated Press; Eric Lee for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Republicans unsuccessfully sought to block a little known candidate, Dan J. Sullivan, left, from the same ballot as their party’s incumbent, Senator Dan S. Sullivan, right.

item.title: La Corte Suprema confirma el fallo: Trump debe pagar 5 millones de dólares por abuso sexual
item.description: El presidente Trump había pedido a los magistrados que intervinieran luego de que un jurado determinó que había abusado sexualmente de la escritora E. Jean Carroll, además de difamarla.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Benjamin Weiser

media:content.media:credit: Sarah Blesener para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: En mayo de 2023, un jurado federal de Nueva York declaró al presidente Trump responsable de haber abusado sexualmente de la escritora E. Jean Carroll y de haberla difamado.

item.title: Kean Set to Speak at the Capitol After Mysterious Absence
item.description: After months of silence, Representative Tom Kean Jr. was expected to deliver a speech explaining his 116-day absence from Congress.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni

media:content.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Representative Tom Kean Jr. in Bridgewater, N.J., in 2024.

item.title: Senator Gallego’s Use of Campaign Funds for Family Trips Prompts Scrutiny
item.description: The Arizona senator used campaign money to fly his family to Nantucket and the Caribbean. The Justice Department has said it is investigating his campaign finance activity.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Kellen Browning

media:content.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Senator Ruben Gallego of Arizona repeatedly took his family to vacation destinations and billed tens of thousands of dollars in flights to his campaign accounts just in 2025.

item.title: How the Wildfires in Colorado and Utah Were Poised to Spark
item.description: Since last week, meteorologists issued a series of increasingly dire warnings about extremely critical weather conditions.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

media:content.media:credit: Noah Berger/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: The Snyder fire burning near Thompson Springs, Utah, on Sunday. It was one of several blazes across the Southwest.

item.title: Mamdani Nears Deal to Resolve Budget Fight Over Housing Vouchers
item.description: The dispute between Mayor Zohran Mamdani and his occasional rival, City Council Speaker Julie Menin, has held up passage of New York City’s $125 billion budget.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein, Sally Goldenberg and Mihir Zaveri

media:content.media:credit: Kent J. Edwards for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani was against expanding a rental assistance program because of its impact on the city’s budget.

item.title: Trump on Legislation to Address the Nation’s Housing Crisis: ‘It’s a Yawn’
item.description: Time and again, President Trump has brushed off Americans’ concerns about the economy and their financial situations.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh

media:content.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump said on Monday that he had yet to receive a bill intended to lower housing costs and that he was not sure he would sign it once he did.

item.title: Keiko Fujimori gana las elecciones presidenciales en Perú
item.description: Keiko Fujimori, hija del exdictador Alberto Fujimori, devuelve al movimiento de su familia al poder, aunque con una victoria ajustada en un país dividido.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky

media:content.media:credit: Anthony Nino De Guzman/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Keiko Fujimori saludaba a sus seguidores durante su mitin de clausura de campaña en Lima, la capital de Perú, en junio. Se ha convertido en la primera mujer electa para ocupar el cargo más alto de Perú.

item.title: What to Watch in Colorado Primary Elections: Will Left-Wing Energy Keep Rising?
item.description: Primaries on Tuesday will be the latest test of Democrats’ anti-establishment mood.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

media:content.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times; David Zalubowski/Associated Press

item.title: Search for Venezuela Quake Survivors Enters Fifth Day
item.description: Hopes of finding earthquake survivors has faded in Venezuela as the search entered its fifth day on Monday. The official death toll had risen to over 1,700 as of Monday afternoon, according to local authorities.
item.dc:creator: Cynthia Silva and Daniel Fetherston

media:content.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: At La Guaira port, the authorities have set up an improvised morgue for relatives to recognize the bodies of the earthquake’s victims in Venezuela, on Monday.

item.title: The Supreme Court Expands Presidential Power
item.description: Also, NASA plans to catch a falling space telescope and nudge it up. Here’s the latest at the end of Monday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times

item.title: In Peru’s Presidential Election, Keiko Fujimori Gives the Right in Latin America Another Win
item.description: Keiko Fujimori, a daughter of the former strongman Alberto Fujimori, returns her family’s movement to power, but with a narrow victory in a divided country.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky

media:content.media:credit: Anthony Nino De Guzman/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Keiko Fujimori waving to supporters during her closing campaign rally in Peru’s capital, Lima, in June. She has become the first woman to hold Peru’s highest office.

item.title: Antes de los terremotos en Venezuela, millones recibieron alertas en sus celulares
item.description: Un sistema de detección sísmica basado en teléfonos celulares identificó dos terremotos en Venezuela y envió advertencias tempranas a usuarios en la región, incluso antes de que se sintieran con toda su intensidad.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff and Martín González Gómez

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: The Pursuit of Hungriness: 250 Years of American Food Innovation
item.description: Sliced bread. Soul food. Cheetos. Here are 25 inventions, crazes and cravings that defined the national diet, decade by decade.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson

media:content.media:credit: Universal History Archive/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Mulberry Street in Manhattan’s Little Italy was a commercial hub for the more than four million Italians who immigrated to the United States by 1920.

item.title: Shooting in Northern Germany Kills 6
item.description: A shooting at a youth center in northern German left six people dead and several others injured, the local authorities said. The police arrested the alleged gunman and another individual connected to the shooting.
item.dc:creator: Meg Felling

media:content.media:credit: Ibrahim Ot/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: The scene where five people were killed on Monday in a shooting in Stade, in northern Germany. A sixth victim died at a hospital.

item.title: Read Newly Discovered Documents from John Dickinson, a U.S. Founding Father
item.description: A letter from Dickinson, a leader of the First and Second Continental Congress, glimpses an effort to peaceably avert the Revolutionary War.

item.title: Emily Barker Loved Fighting Fires. She Died in One She Couldn’t Escape.
item.description: Emily Barker was one of two women who died fighting a large forest fire in Colorado this week.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy

media:content.media:credit: via Lisa Karczewski
media:content.media:description: Emily Barker loved the outdoors and crossed the country doing prescribed burns and fighting fires each summer.

item.title: What the U.S. Owes Venezuela
item.description: Expectations are rising over the role the Trump administration could play in helping after two devastating quakes.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Volunteers in Caracas loaded donations and supplies to deliver to earthquake survivors on Friday.

item.title: Drone Strikes JetBlue Flight Landing at Kennedy Airport, Pilot Says
item.description: The incident Monday came less than three days after a United Airlines crew reported encountering a drone while arriving at Newark Liberty International Airport.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan

media:content.media:credit: Yuki IWAMURA / AFP
media:content.media:description: No injuries or damage were reported on Monday after a JetBlue flight landing at Kennedy International Airport was hit by a drone.

item.title: Man Indicted on Charges of Stabbing 7 People at Penn Station
item.description: Prosecutors say the man, Hector Deleon, went on a spree of random violence in June at the nation’s busiest transit hub, leaving at least one person with a brain injury.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman

media:content.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The random stabbing of seven people earlier this month sowed panic at Penn Station, the nation’s busiest transit hub.

item.title: Abdul Ahad Momand, Only Afghan to Fly in Space, Is Dead
item.description: A pilot in the Afghan Air Force, he was recruited for the Soviet space program. His 1988 flight took place as the Soviets were fighting a war in his country.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: George Gresham, Health Care Union Powerhouse, Dies at 71
item.description: Until he lost an election last year amid accusations of self-enrichment, he was a longtime influential force in Democratic politics in New York.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

media:content.media:credit: Suzanne DeChillo/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: George Gresham in 2007, when he became the president of Local 1199 of the Service Employees International Union, the largest health care workers union in the United States.

item.title: Las mejores películas de 2026, hasta ahora
item.description: Si aún no las has visto, este es un buen momento para ponerte al día. Estas son las producciones favoritas de nuestros críticos.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman

item.title: Supreme Court Expands Presidential Powers to Fire Independent Regulators
item.description: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that President Trump could fire independent regulators for any reason. But the justices carved out an exception for the Federal Reserve, preventing the immediate removal of Lisa D. Cook, a Federal Reserve governor.
item.dc:creator: McKinnon de Kuyper

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Supreme Court ruling has implications for more than two dozen agencies that have traditionally been insulated from presidential control.

item.title: La ciencia detrás de los alimentos fermentados
item.description: Los investigadores empiezan a comprender cómo alimentos como el kimchi, el yogur y el chucrut influyen en la salud.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan

media:content.media:credit: Celeste Noche para The New York Times

item.title: Daveigh Chase, ‘Lilo & Stitch’ Voice Actor, Died of AIDS, Officials Say
item.description: Ms. Chase was the voice of Lilo and also played the child villain in the horror film “The Ring.” She died in Los Angeles this month.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens

media:content.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Getty Images North America
media:content.media:description: The actress Daveigh Chase in 2011. Her father said she was homeless before she died this month in Los Angeles.
